name: Yale
description: The Yale is a mythical beast described in European bestiaries and heraldry. It is typically depicted as an antelope-like or goat-like creature with swiveling horns. These horns, which can move independently, are its most distinctive feature, allowing it to defend itself from multiple attackers simultaneously. The Yale is noted for its size and strength, and often symbolizes pride, defense, and sometimes even aggression due to its formidable horns. Mentions of the Yale can be traced back to Pliny the Elder, who described it as an Aethiopian creature. Its inclusion in bestiaries and heraldry cemented its place in European folklore, where it is frequently associated with royalty and nobility. In heraldry, the Yale is often used as a supporter of arms, particularly in the arms of the British Royal Family, where it represents a symbol of strength and vigilance.
subtitle: A mythical antelope-like beast with swiveling horns.
appearance: The Yale is typically depicted as having the body of a goat or antelope, with a dark or tawny coat. Its most distinguishing feature is its pair of large horns that can swivel independently, allowing it to aim them in different directions. It often has cloven hooves and a goat-like beard. Depictions vary, but it is generally portrayed as a muscular and powerful creature.
habitat: Mythical bestiaries place the Yale in various regions, often associating it with wilderness areas, mountains, and regions of Africa, particularly Ethiopia, as described by Pliny the Elder.
behavior: The Yale is described as being fiercely independent and protective of its territory. Its swiveling horns make it a formidable opponent, capable of defending itself against multiple attackers. It is generally considered a proud and vigilant creature, representing strength and resilience.

keyMyths.title: Pliny the Elder's Description
keyMyths.description: Pliny the Elder, in his Natural History, provides one of the earliest known descriptions of the Yale, referring to it as a creature from Aethiopia. He highlights its unique ability to move its horns, making it a formidable opponent. This account is foundational to the Yale's subsequent representation in bestiaries.
keyMyths.source: Pliny the Elder, Natural History, Book VIII

keyMyths.title: The Yale in Heraldry
keyMyths.description: The Yale gained prominence in heraldry, particularly in British heraldry, where it is used as a supporter of arms. It symbolizes strength, vigilance, and nobility. Its inclusion in royal heraldry underscores its association with power and prestige. The Yale's presence signifies the bearer's qualities of defense and resilience.
keyMyths.source: Arthur Charles Fox-Davies, A Complete Guide to Heraldry

keyMyths.title: Bestiary Accounts
keyMyths.description: Medieval bestiaries often included the Yale, drawing from Pliny's account and embellishing its characteristics. These texts emphasized its unusual horns and defensive capabilities. The bestiaries served to popularize the Yale as a creature of myth and legend, contributing to its ongoing presence in folklore and art.
keyMyths.source: T.H. White, The Book of Beasts

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Provides one of the earliest known descriptions of the Yale, highlighting its unique horn movement.
